extract_meta.description: Session B findings with catalogue question mapping — generic findings only

findings.finding: Reg 56 (envy) holds the full positive-negative dual register of the zeal/jealousy vocabulary. The same inner passion (consuming zealous desire) appears as: (a) destructive human jealousy/envy (Affective/Emotional, Moral/Conscience), (b) positive human zeal for God (Spiritual/God-ward), (c) divine jealousy as exclusive covenantal claim (Theological/Divine-Human). The inner capacity is identical; direction of passion determines valence. Session B should map this tripartite structure and ask what makes the same inner passion sanctified or destructive.

Dialogismos (G1261) displays the full range of its directionally-determined character in C02: 1081-001 names corrupt inner reasoning originating in the heart (Moral/Conscience) and 1081-002 names the mind's legitimate deliberation on contested matters (Cognitive/Mind). Same root, same faculty, opposite moral orientation. Session B should treat dialogismos as a directionally-determined term whose dimension shifts with context.

The sur (to turn aside) sub-gloss groups in Reg 128 show a consistent pattern: turning aside can be from God (6076-001 — Spiritual/God-ward negative), from idols toward God (6075-001 — Spiritual/God-ward positive), or toward God (6076-003 — Spiritual/God-ward positive). The same volitional act (turning) is the mechanism for both rebellion and repentance — only the direction differs. Session B should examine whether the turning vocabulary illuminates rebellion as fundamentally a directional problem of the inner being, and what this implies for understanding repentance as the reversal of the same inner movement.

Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout.

Registry 123 (pride) carries the most analytically complex dual-register in the cluster: human pride and divine majesty share the same vocabulary roots (ga.ah, ga.on, rum, ra.mam). The kauchaomai (26-001/002/003) groups make the structure explicit: legitimate boasting is directed toward God (Spiritual/God-ward); illegitimate boasting turns inward or toward self (Character/Disposition). The biblical claim is that pride is the human attempt to claim for oneself what belongs to God's majesty alone. Session B should examine the pride/majesty dual-register as a structured theological claim about the relationship between divine transcendence and human self-assertion.

Registry 2 (agony) contains two analytically distinct sub-clusters: (1) the agōn- word family (6 terms: G0075, G0073, G0464, G1864, G2610, G4865), all Volitional/Will, naming directed spiritual striving rather than suffering — these are misaligned with C05's dominant suffering theme; (2) the basanizō/basanismos/basanos torment family (5 terms), all Affective/Emotional, naming eschatological and post-mortem suffering. Session B should treat these sub-clusters separately.

Reg 35 contains two analytically distinct sub-clusters: (1) willing-spirit/eager-disposition (prothumia, prothumos, prothumōs, euthumeō, euthumos, athumeō) and (2) covetous-desire proper (pleonexia). Session B should treat these as distinct inner-being phenomena requiring separate analytical attention.

The Greek division vocabulary in the division registry (schizō G4977, schisma G4978, merizō G3307, diamerizō G1266, meris G3310, meros G3313) shows a differentiated inner-being landscape: schisma/schizō name communal fracture over identity evaluation; merizō names internal volitional division; diamerizō names interpersonal fracture from ultimate allegiance; meris/meros name relational belonging and volitional orientation toward Christ. Session B should examine these terms in relation to each other and to the Hebrew portion vocabulary (chelek, cheleq, chalaq).

The will registry shows the widest dimension spread in C14, spanning Volitional/Will, Theological/Divine-Human, Affective/Emotional, Spiritual/God-ward, Character/Disposition, Cognitive/Mind, Identity/Selfhood, Moral/Conscience. Session B must begin with sub-cluster taxonomy: which groups name the volitional act itself, which name surrounding conditions, and which entered via functional/grammatical terms.

The worship registry contains three distinct sub-clusters worth separate Session B attention: (1) proskuneō/shachah — prostration as the physical-inner act of submission/devotion; (2) avodah/latreia — service/ministry as vocation; (3) non-worship XREF vocabulary (pit, dog, work, bondage). Session B should focus primarily on (1) and (2) as the core worship inner-being domain.

Misdirected desire dominates a large segment of Registry 43 — 10+ groups (464-001, 469-001, 480-001, 481-001, 481-003, 492-003, 497-001, 498-001, 504-001, 505-001) name desire as moral disorder. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats desire's moral valence as its primary theological concern, and how the disordered and rightly-ordered forms relate.

Group 26-003 (Paul's paradoxical boasting in weakness — sustained inner act of inverting normal pride to reveal divine power) is analytically distinctive. This is not the absence of pride but its structural transformation: weakness embraced as the vehicle through which God's power is displayed. This group bridges C09 (pride/boasting) and C08 (Reg 170 weakness). Session B should examine this as a distinct inner-being posture: the voluntary inversion of the pride-orientation as itself a form of God-ward inner act.

Reg 181 (zeal/spoudē) is uniformly positive — lacking the negative jealousy/envy register entirely. Dimension profile: Character/Disposition (3), Spiritual/God-ward (1), Volitional/Will (1). This contrasts with Reg 56 zeal vocabulary (predominantly Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward, both positive and negative valence). Session B should analyse what distinguishes earnest diligence (spoudē, Reg 181) from consuming passion (zēlos, Reg 56) — whether vocabulary difference, semantic field distinction, or theological claim about godly zeal.

Registry 43 (desire) shows a consistent divine/human register split: divine desire, will, and longing = Theological/Divine-Human; human desire and will = Volitional/Will or desiderative. These two registers appear throughout the vocabulary. Session B should read them in parallel and assess how Scripture's account of divine desire relates to human desire.

Five groups in the division registry are assigned Divine-Human Correspondence (2962-002, 5274-002, 5280-001, 824-001, 828-001). This is a significant concentration. The portion/lot vocabulary (chelek, chalaq, meros, meris) consistently operates in divine-human correspondence mode — God allots, human receives, inner states arise in response. Session B should examine whether this correspondence structure is intrinsic to the portion concept or a feature of the particular biblical corpus.

Registry 135 shows a consistent human-divine mirroring structure: nearly every human inner-being act (turning, relenting, comforting, recalling) has a divine counterpart in the same registry (God relenting, God comforting, God withholding wrath). Session B should analyse this pairing as a key structural feature of the repentance domain in Scripture.

All seven whoredom groups are dominated by spiritual/covenantal whoredom — Israel's heart-departure toward foreign gods. The literal sexual dimension appears in only one group (1221-004). Session B should examine how the spiritual whoredom metaphor relates to the literal sexual meaning as inner-being phenomena, and whether covenantal faithfulness is the primary inner-being register of this vocabulary in Scripture.

The blessing vocabulary reveals a mirror structure in Relational Disposition: group 1299-001 names God's inner disposition of love as the source of his blessing toward persons (GOD dominant); group 1299-002 names the human inner orientation of worship and praise as the act of blessing God (HUMAN dominant). Session B should examine this mutual relational structure — whether the blessing vocabulary discloses a covenantal reciprocity in inner-being orientation between God and persons, and what this reveals about the inner being as the site of covenant relationship.

Comfort vocabulary splits cleanly: God-given comfort (Spiritual/God-ward) and community-given comfort (Relational/Social). Paraklēsis spans both dimensions across its three groups. Session B should examine the structural relationship between divine and human comfort — the data suggests divine comfort both enables and models the human comfort that flows from it.

Three groups in the image registry (1359-001 tse.lem, 1360-001 de.mut, 1361-001 eikōn) all assign Divine-Human Correspondence to the imago Dei statement under three different terms. This convergence across Hebrew tse.lem, Hebrew de.mut, and Greek eikōn is analytically significant: all three terms independently arrive at the same dimension for the same verse (Gen 1:26-27). Session B should examine the imago Dei as the ontological foundation of Divine-Human Correspondence throughout the programme — whether the image-bearing of the human person is the root from which all other divine-human correspondences in the inner being flow.

Registry 184 has the highest Theological/Divine-Human density of any C01 registry: 17 of 37 groups (46%) assigned Theological/Divine-Human. Session B: the spirit vocabulary is inherently relational — the human spirit exists primarily in the context of God's Spirit. This distinguishes spirit from soul and heart in the C01 structural analysis.

Genesis 3:10 (group 7414-001 — e.rom) is the foundational inner-being text for the vulnerability cluster: 'I was afraid, because I was naked, and I hid myself.' This encodes the primary inner-being sequence of moral failure: nakedness-awareness → fear → self-concealment. Session B should examine how this three-stage sequence (exposure of inner condition → negative emotional response → volitional self-concealment from God) structures the anthropology of inner vulnerability across the OT and NT. The contrast with Gen 2:25 (naked and not ashamed) establishes the pre-fall / post-fall inner-being contrast as a structural framework for the vulnerability cluster.

Group 513-001 names the divided inner will — the person wills good but does what they do not will (Romans 7 pattern). This is analytically foundational for understanding desire's relationship to action. Session B should give this sustained attention as a key inner-being mechanism.

Group 1374-001 (the sluggard whose desire craves but whose will refuses to act) names sloth's inner-being structure precisely: not the absence of desire but the uncoupling of desire from volitional action. This is the inner-being gap that defines sloth — desire without will. Session B should examine whether the sloth vocabulary in Reg 208 consistently identifies this desire/will gap, or whether sloth is also described as the absence of desire itself, and what this reveals about the relationship between desire, will, and action in the biblical anthropology.

Registry 170 (weakness) is analytically anomalous within C09 (vice cluster). The weakness vocabulary in Paul shows a structured theological inversion: weakness is simultaneously a limitation (Affective/Emotional, 1219-001) and the privileged inner-being condition in which divine power is revealed (Theological/Divine-Human, 1219-002; 6639-003), God elects (Theological/Divine-Human, 6639-003), and Christ's pattern is enacted. Session B should examine weakness as a paradoxical inner-being category and ask whether it belongs analytically within C09 or whether it represents a distinct inner-being posture better addressed in relation to C08 (endurance) or C15 (faith/trust).

The delight vocabulary spans every dimension of the inner being. This breadth suggests delight is not a single category but a family of engagements organised around the axis of what the inner person is oriented toward. Session B analysis should map this orientation axis as the structuring principle of the registry.

cha.zaq (H2388G) group 4923-002 ('Be strong and courageous' grounded in divine presence) is the paradigmatic biblical courage formula: courage is not self-generated but is a response to and product of divine accompaniment. The command and the promise of presence are structurally inseparable. Session B should examine cha.zaq's courage vocabulary as the primary inner-being theological claim of Reg 33: courage as relational-received-from-God, not as an autonomous character virtue.

The name registry (Reg 204) shows a consistent Hebrew-Greek parallel structure: 1366-001/1367-001 (divine name as self-disclosure), 1366-002/1367-002 (human orientation through the divine name), 1366-003/1367-003 (name as moral character), 1366-004/1367-004 (naming as identity transformation). The same inner-being structure appears under both shem and onoma. Session B should examine whether this structural parallel reflects a unified understanding of naming as inner-being constitutive act across the Testaments, or whether there are genuine differences between the Hebrew and Greek registers.

Registry 124 (prophecy) shows a consistent pattern of Agency/Power (Spirit-enabled capacity exercised) alongside Relational Disposition (inner orientation toward God or his word). Session B should examine the relationship between inner capacity and inner orientation in the prophetic person: is prophetic capacity a dimension in its own right, or is it always constituted by and in service of a prior relational orientation toward God?

Three groups within the covenant registry show the new covenant as inner transformation (765-003, 766-002, 777-001 — law written on heart, new covenant through Spirit, new self/creation in Christ). Session B should examine the covenant registry's inner transformation dimension: how the trajectory from the Sinai covenant (obedience required externally, inner-being orientation demanded) to the new covenant (transformation from within, law on heart) is encoded in the Dimension Review data and what this discloses about the inner person as the site of covenantal fulfilment.

ne.phesh groups 1381-008 (Servant's soul poured out in self-offering, Isa 53) and 1392-002 (psuchē life laid down in love, Christ for the sheep) together form the atonement-love axis in the soul registry. Both assigned Theological/Divine-Human. Session B: these are the programme's primary soul-and-atonement texts — connect to existing SD flags 182-F005 (Isa 53 atonement) and PH2-182-005 (eschatological soul-substitution).

Soul (#182) carries 1 term with status=phase2_enrichment: H5317 (nophet/honey, 5 active verses). Pro 27:7 — one who is full loathes honey; to one who is hungry everything bitter is sweet — encodes the appetite dimension of nephesh through honey as archetype metaphor. Deliberately excluded from VCB — no verse_context records exist. Active verses remain unflagged by design. Decision carried forward from prior Session B. Session B DataPrep must include this term as contextual support for understanding the soul's appetitive dimension. Verses: Psa 19:10, Pro 5:3, Pro 24:13, Pro 27:7, Song 4:11.

findings.pattern: P03 — Site of both indwelling and corruption
findings.finding: qe.rev (H7130H) spans five groups across five dimensions: Theological/Divine-Human (God's indwelling in the midst), Moral/Conscience (seat of moral character; seat of hostile intent; location of false prophets' lies), Theological/Divine-Human (location of new covenant renewal). Session B: the inner parts as the site of both divine indwelling and moral corruption — a structural inner-being tension at the heart of the heart registry.

Anger governance is the paradigmatic inner-being governance challenge in Reg 4. Groups 79-004 (mastery of anger exceeds military conquest), 1554-001 (trembling restraint before God without sin), 38-001 (morally accountable anger under divine command), and 139-003 (wisdom counsel not to let anger kindle) show anger mastery as a distinct and critical sub-theme. Session B should map the vocabulary of anger-governance as a structured inner-being claim about will, character, and spiritual posture.

Two groups (1552-001: fierce anger co-existing with grief/loyalty; 37-003: righteous anger co-existing with sorrow at hardness of heart) show the inner being holding anger and love, or anger and grief, simultaneously as genuine compound affective states. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats compound anger-with-grief as a distinct inner-being mode and what theological conditions produce it.

merimnaō (G3309) operates in two distinct inner-being registers: anxious affective worry (2709-001, 2709-002) and volitional-attentional directed care (2709-003, 350-001). Session B should map how the same word spans these two registers and what this reveals about anxiety as both an affective state and a directional inner-being act.

Awe spans both Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward dimensions. God-ward groups (1682-001, 1682-003, 703-002, 703-003) show that the deepest fear/awe content is Spiritual/God-ward rather than merely affective. Session B should examine whether awe functions as a distinct inner-being category integrating affective intensity and God-ward orientation — the two are inseparable in the data.

Bitterness in Reg 13 operates in two structural registers: (a) affective quality — bitter grief, anguish, affliction (something that happens to the inner person), and (b) dispositional-character state — root of bitterness, bitter jealousy as heart-state, bitter rebellion (something the inner person becomes). Session B should examine whether biblical bitterness is primarily an experience or a condition, and whether the two registers are sequential or independent.

The ka.vod (glory) vocabulary in Reg 15 (boastfulness) contains its own internal corrective: divine glory (1861-001) as the proper object of inner awe, and the human act of directing glory toward God (1861-002) as the antidote to boastfulness that claims glory for itself. Session B should examine how the glory vocabulary frames the proper and improper objects of inner honour — why the same word (kavod) names both human boastfulness and divine majesty, and what this reveals about the inner-being structure of worship.

parrēsia (G3954/3955) shows a tripartite inner-being structure: (1) access to God in prayer/worship (Spiritual/God-ward, 713-001), (2) apostolic proclamation against opposition (Character/Disposition + Spiritual/God-ward, 713-002, 714-001), (3) relational openness (Relational/Social, 713-003). Boldness is contextually differentiated — its nature shifts with its object and context. Session B should map how boldness-as-God-ward-access, boldness-as-prophetic-character, and boldness-as-relational-quality relate in the inner being.

Registry 46 (devotion) centres on haplotēs/haplous — singleness, undivided-ness, wholeness of inner orientation. The primary analytical claim is that devotion is not primarily an emotional state (fervour) but a structural condition of the inner being: the undivided attention of the whole person toward God. The distraction group (5034-001) names the failure mode: the inner being pulled and scattered. Session B should examine singleness-of-heart as a central inner-being category distinct from both the zeal vocabulary of C07 and the mere performance of worship.

Registry 142 (self-control) shows the self-governance vocabulary operating in three registers: (a) human self-control as character virtue (1106-001, 1113-001, 6203-001), (b) human volitional exercise of restraint (1107-001, 6208-001, 1109-001, 1111-001), (c) Spirit-gift of self-discipline as divine endowment (1112-001). The human effort and the divine gift coexist and the vocabulary does not sharply distinguish them. Session B should examine whether biblical self-control is primarily a virtue cultivated, a gift received, or an exercise of will — or whether all three are held in tension as aspects of the same inner-being governance.

The new covenant transformation group (3272-001: diatithēmi — the covenantal act writing divine law on the inner mind and heart) appears in the abomination registry. This places the new covenant's inner transformation as the divine answer to abomination and inner defilement. Session B should examine whether the abomination cluster's verse evidence shows the new covenant specifically as the remedy for abomination — and whether this pattern recurs across the cluster.

apobolē (1094-001, Rom 11:15) carries one of the highest concentrations of theological density in the programme — rejection, reconciliation, and life from the dead in a single verse. The group is assigned Divine-Human Correspondence with GOD as dominant subject. Session B should examine the inner-being dynamic of Israel's covenantal rejection: what the inner-relational experience of being cast off by God means for the human inner person, and how it relates to the redemptive correspondence that Paul draws.

Group 1361-002 (eikōn — 2 Cor 3:18, 'transformed into the same image from glory to glory') is assigned Transformation. This group is structurally adjacent to the three imago Dei groups but names a process rather than a state — the inner person being actively changed toward the divine image. Session B should examine the relationship between bearing the image (static ontological correspondence) and being transformed toward it (dynamic process) — these may be two aspects of a single inner-being reality that need to be held together.

findings.pattern: P10 — Two forms of same word
findings.finding: The submission registry distinguishes two dimensions: relational submission to human authority ([1147-001], Relational/Social) and submission before God ([1148-001], Spiritual/God-ward). Session B should examine whether this distinction is sustained across the broader submission/surrender/yielding vocabulary in C14.

Contempt vocabulary in Registry 190 operates at three levels: (a) the inner disposition of contempt as evaluative stance (Character/Disposition), (b) contempt as social-relational dynamic (Relational/Social), (c) contempt with theological weight directed at God, his word, or his servant (Moral/Conscience, Theological/Divine-Human). Session B should map how these three registers of contempt relate in the inner being.

Shame vocabulary in Registry 146 operates on two consistent axes: (a) inner shame as felt condition (affective, moral, spiritual) and (b) shame as socially-imposed or relationally-enacted condition (relational, theological). The same Hebrew/Greek words move between these axes. Session B should map how Scripture relates the inner experience of shame to its social construction and how these axes intersect in the biblical theology of honour/shame.

Registry 6 (anointing) spans 7 dimensions across 38 groups — the widest dimensional range in C16. Session B should examine whether this breadth coheres around a unifying theme (Spirit-mediated transformation and divine endowment) or represents genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena requiring separate analytical threads.

Multiple Transformation groups in Reg 6 have GOD as dominant subject (167-001, 179-002, 4697-001, 4701-001, 4701-002). Session B should explore the full theology of God-initiated inner transformation in the anointing vocabulary — spanning Spirit-impartation, forgiveness, restoration, eschatological renewal, and judicial dulling/hardening as distinct expressions of the same divine transforming agency.

The aletheia word family spans five distinct inner-being dimensions across 10 groups: Spiritual/God-ward, Volitional/Will, Character/Disposition, Theological/Divine-Human, Cognitive/Mind. No single dimension captures the full range. Session B should examine whether 'truthfulness' is the right registry label, and whether the term's biblical range is better described as 'truth-orientation' or another formulation that captures both the character quality and the transformational/pneumatological dimensions.

The darkness vocabulary (skotos, skotia, skoteinos, skotizō, skotoō) generates multiple distinct inner-being characteristics across its groups: identity condition (7432-001, 7433-001), moral domain (7432-002), concealment/cognition (7432-003, 7433-002), eschatological consequence (7432-004), whole-person darkening (7435-001), and active darkening of faculties (7434-001). This is a term family with unusually wide inner-being range. Session B should assess whether these groups reflect a coherent single inner-being phenomenon (darkness as multi-dimensional spiritual reality) or whether the groupings require further subdivision.

The la/lo negation terms (H3809, H3808) build inner-being content through naming what is absent, refused, or withheld. The word does not carry inner-being content directly — it reveals inner states by negating them. Session B should examine this structural inversion as a distinctive biblical rhetoric for naming despair, faith-failure, and inner collapse.

findings.pattern: P12 — Stable condition vs. transient state
findings.finding: Both contentment terms name a stable inner condition independent of circumstances — a learned/acquired disposition of sufficiency, not a transient emotional state. Session B should examine whether contentment constitutes a distinct sub-dimension of Character/Disposition, and how it relates to the Philippians 4 context where it appears alongside joy and peace.

The seeking registry contains seeking vocabulary (darash, shachar) that is among the most intense inner-being orientation language in Scripture — the soul yearning for God. Session B should examine seeking as a specific form of Spiritual/God-ward orientation distinct from faith (trust-reliance) and worship (devotional service) — seeking names the active inner pursuit of encounter with God.

The trust registry shows Character/Disposition as the dominant dimension — trust as a stable inner posture of reliance rather than primarily an act. Session B should examine the relationship between trust-as-posture (Character/Disposition) and faith-as-act (Volitional/Will in faith registry) — two complementary ways Scripture names the inner orientation toward God.

The tahor/taher word family in the purity registry spans ritual cleansing (external covenantal state), moral purification (inner), and divine restorative action. Session B should map how the ritual and moral dimensions relate — and whether the programme treats purity primarily as an inner character quality or as a covenantal status granted and maintained through prescribed practice.

The conscience (suneidesis/sunoida) groups in Reg 26 show a consistent pattern: moral self-awareness operating under divine authority — conscience as inner witness that remains subordinate to God's judgment. Session B should examine the relationship between conscience-as-moral-witness and divine judgment as the higher court.

apoballō (6115-001) covers only two NT verses (Heb 10:35 and Mark 10:50) — one warning against throwing away confidence, one depicting urgent casting aside of impediment toward Christ. These are inverse volitional acts: one guarding an inner disposition, one releasing an external obstacle. Session B should examine whether these two uses illuminate a single inner-being faculty (the will's relationship to what it holds or releases) or represent genuinely different phenomena.

Group 1286-001 (H2963 ta.raph, tearing) was assigned Emotion — Negative / HUMAN to capture the human experience of violent inner states: wrath, predatory character, destructive anger, and the suffering of being torn. The same group contains significant GOD-dimension content — divine wrath and fierce resolve to fight for his people. Session B should study both dimensions: (1) the human inner-being experience of violent states and of being torn under judgment or hostile power; and (2) the divine inner-being reality of wrath and fierce protective resolve, exploring whether these are the same phenomenon viewed from different subjects or whether they name distinct inner-being realities.

Registry 160 (thought) contains three instances of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern: (1) re.a (H7454) — 3453-001 names thoughts known by God and God's thoughts precious to the psalmist, the same relational faculty operating from both sides of the divine-human boundary simultaneously (Sub-pattern C); (2) dialogismos (G1261) — 3409-001 names inner thoughts as site of spiritual warfare and accusation (Moral/Conscience) and 3410-001 names inner reckoning as the ground of faith (Cognitive/Mind) — same root, opposite orientation; (3) fronimos (G5429) pattern carries from C01: prudence and craftiness as the same perceptive faculty (Sub-pattern B). Session B must treat these as analytically distinct uses of the same terms.

Registry 31 contains two distinct sub-domains: (1) moral corruption as inner condition (4899-001, 4900-001, 4901-001, 745-001) and (2) eschatological/thanatological corruption (Hades, Gehenna, Sheol, resurrection hope — assigned Theological/Divine-Human or Spiritual/God-ward). Session B should map how the moral and eschatological uses of corruption relate — do they share a common inner-being logic or are they genuinely distinct?

Identity/Selfhood groups in the Soul registry (1385-001 human as living soul, 1391-001 psuchē as essential inner person, 1396-001/2 psuchikos as natural mode, 1400-001 apsuchos as negation) together define what the soul is constitutively. Session B: these form the ontological foundation of Registry 182 — the soul as a mode of being, not merely a function.

pneuma (G4151G) spans seven groups covering seven distinct inner-being functions: Holy Spirit transforming presence, human spirit as experiential seat, spirit-flesh contrast, unclean spirit, spirit-characterised orientations, life-giving Spirit, spiritual gifts. This is the most dimensionally diverse single term in C01. Session B: the spirit vocabulary in NT is the analytical heart of Registry 184 — mapping its full range requires the complete seven-group reading.

Group 1216-006 (ya.shar — 'God made man upright, but they have sought out many schemes', Eccl. 7:29) is the only group in C10 explicitly addressing the original moral constitution of the human person and its departure. Session B should treat this as a significant anthropological statement and examine its implications for understanding uprightness as a creational category within the inner-being framework.

Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately.

Group 7013-001 (a.yil, ram) carries a verse-level valuation in which obedience (H8085H sha.ma) and attentive listening (H7181 qashav) are declared greater than sacrifice — greater than the fat of rams (1 Sam 15:22). Neither term is an OWNER in Registry 187; their inner-being weight is present in the verse but unregistered in the programme. Session B for Registry 187 should explore: (1) what the comparative structure of 1 Sam 15:22 reveals about the hierarchy of inner-being orientations toward God — obedience and attentive listening as the higher register; (2) how this valuation relates to the strength/capacity cluster's broader understanding of inner-being agency before God; (3) the relationship between sha.ma and qashav as paired inner-being acts that together constitute the responsive orientation of the person toward God.
